task: I’m putting together a simple process for handling small campaign mistakes or misses so they don’t turn into the same problem twice. Can you help me sketch a practical way to review what went wrong and update the workflow after the fact?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the simplest way is to do a short **post-miss review** and turn the fix into a process change.

## 1) Capture what happened
Write down:
- what missed
- when it was caught
- who noticed it
- what it affected
- whether it went live or was caught first

## 2) Identify the break point
Ask:
- was it a copy issue?
- timing issue?
- approval issue?
- handoff issue?
- tracking / QA issue?
- ownership issue?

## 3) Find the root cause
Keep asking “why” until you get to the real reason:
- unclear owner
- no cutoff
- stale source of truth
- missing QA step
- too many handoffs
- late change without recheck

## 4) Decide the fix
Pick one concrete change:
- add a checklist step
- assign one owner
- add a cutoff time
- require re-QA after edits
- tighten approval flow
- update the tracker

## 5) Update the workflow
Put the fix into:
- the tracker
- the SOP
- the checklist
- the handoff note

## 6) Confirm it sticks
For the next similar task, check whether the same miss happens again. If it does, the fix wasn’t specific enough.

---

## Simple review format
- **Issue:**  
- **Impact:**  
- **Root cause:**  
- **Fix:**  
- **Owner:**  
- **New rule:**  
- **Date added to workflow:**  

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page post-mortem template** you can reuse.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is solid. Not too much fluff.

I’d want one more layer though — make it a little more operational, like:

- **severity**: minor / moderate / major
- **where it broke**: copy, timing, approval, tracker, handoff
- **preventable?** yes / no
- **new control**: exact step added
- **who owns the control**
- **when it gets checked again**

Because if it’s just “root cause” and “fix,” people write vague stuff and nothing changes. I’ve seen that happen. The useful part is making the workflow change hard to ignore.

If you want, I’d want the one-page template to look more like a fill-in form than a paragraph doc.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more operational fill-in template.

# Post-Miss Review Template

## 1) Issue summary
- **Issue:**  
- **Date caught:**  
- **Who caught it:**  
- **What it affected:**  
- **Went live or caught first:**  

## 2) Severity
- **Severity:** minor / moderate / major  
- **Why:**  

## 3) Where it broke
- copy
- timing
- approval
- tracker
- handoff
- QA
- ownership
- other: ______

## 4) Preventable?
- **Preventable?** yes / no  
- **Why:**  

## 5) Root cause
- **Root cause:**  
- **Contributing factors:**  

## 6) New control
- **Exact new step added:**  
- **Where it lives:** tracker / SOP / checklist / handoff note  
- **Who owns it:**  
- **When it gets checked again:**  

## 7) Action items
| Action | Owner | Due date | Status |
|---|---|---:|---|
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

## 8) Closeout rule
Don’t close the review until:
- the new control is written down
- the owner is assigned
- the next check date is set

## Short fill-in version
- **Severity:**  
- **Break point:**  
- **Preventable:**  
- **Root cause:**  
- **New control:**  
- **Owner:**  
- **Recheck date:**  

If you want, I can also turn this into a **Slack-ready post-miss note** you can paste after a campaign miss.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right shape. That closeout rule is the part people usually skip.

One tweak I’d want: make the **new control** field force an actual action, not a vague note. Like:
- “Add final QA by channel owner before send”
- “Require approval in tracker before asset goes live”
- “Update store-hour check at 3pm daily during storm weeks”

And maybe add one line for **reoccurrence risk** or **blast radius** if you want it to stay practical. Sometimes a minor miss on paper is still a big deal if it hits multiple partners.

If you want the next version, I’d make it a clean fill-in form with maybe 6–7 fields max, then a tiny action table at the bottom. That’s usually the sweet spot — enough structure to stop vague writeups, not so much that nobody uses it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter fill-in version.

# Post-Miss Review Form

## 1) Issue details
- **Issue:**  
- **Date caught:**  
- **Who caught it:**  
- **What it affected:**  
- **Went live or caught first:**  

## 2) Severity / blast radius
- **Severity:** minor / moderate / major  
- **Blast radius:** low / medium / high  
- **Why:**  

## 3) Where it broke
- copy
- timing
- approval
- tracker
- handoff
- QA
- ownership
- other: ______

## 4) Preventable?
- **Preventable?** yes / no  
- **Why:**  

## 5) Root cause
- **Root cause:**  
- **Contributing factors:**  

## 6) New control
- **Exact action added:**  
- **Who owns it:**  
- **Where it lives:** tracker / SOP / checklist / handoff note  
- **When it gets checked again:**  

## 7) Action table
| Action | Owner | Due date | Status |
|---|---|---:|---|
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

## 8) Closeout rule
Don’t close the review until:
- the new control is an actual action
- the owner is assigned
- the recheck date is set

## Short version
- **Severity:**  
- **Blast radius:**  
- **Break point:**  
- **Preventable:**  
- **Root cause:**  
- **New control action:**  
- **Owner:**  
- **Recheck date:**  

If you want, I can make this into a **one-screen copy/paste template** for Notes or Slack.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s it. Clean enough to use, but still forces people to write something real instead of “we’ll be more careful.”

The **new control action** field is the important part. That’s what keeps it from turning into vague postmortem language.

This version is usable as-is for me. If anything, I’d just keep the form tight so people actually fill it out after a miss instead of putting it off.
